text: Hello, my name is Jose Pimiento, and I'm one of the surgical oncologists at Moffitt Cancer Center. I have a video for you guys today.
It's the robotic total gastrectomy for cancer in the background of CDH-I mutation.
The case is one of a thirty-year-old with diagnosis of a pathogenic CDH-I mutation in a screening endoscopy. It was found to have foci of invasive signet-ring cell cancer in random biopsies.
No clear family history for invasive gastric cancer and imaging studies did not reveal neural disease or metastasis.
The surgical team places the ports, including a twelve millimeter robotic port for stapling and a twelve millimeter system port designated for extraction and EEA (end-to-end anastomosis).
The surgeon begins by dividing the gastrocolic ligament, including the left gastroepiploic vessel, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect and divide the short gastric vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic artery, which appears redundant, to avoid injury. They proceed to divide the attachments of the greater curvature of the stomach to the diaphragm, facilitating the mobilization of the proximal stomach.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal stomach and dissects the right gastrointestinal vessels, removing the lymph node package. The vessels are divided using a vascular stapler. Attention then shifts to the lesser curve, where the right gastric vessels are dissected and divided using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon carefully dissects and divides the duodenum, using a blue load stapler to complete the division while avoiding injury to the surrounding tissue. A small serosal tear is visible during the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the pars flaccida and identifies the right crux, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully removes lymph nodes associated with the celiac trunk and left gastric artery, fully exposing the anterior surface of both the right and left crura. The lymph node package is removed, and attention is then directed to the takeoff of the splenic artery, including associated lymphatics.

The surgeon dissects the left gastric vessels, leaving the hepatic artery lymph nodes intact for later removal. The left gastric vessels are divided using a vascular stapler, with careful attention to avoid narrowing the celiac trunk.

The surgeon fully dissects the hiatus, freeing the esophagus while carefully avoiding injury to the intrathoracic esophagus intended for anastomosis. Once the esophagus is exposed and dissected, it is divided using a blue load stapler. The freed specimen, including the omentum, is placed in a large endocash bag. The proximal margin containing the esophagus and the distal margin containing the duodenum are prepared for frozen section analysis.
The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy of the hepatic artery periportal territory using a vessel sealer, carefully excising lymphatic tissue and placing it in a separate specimen retrieval bag.

The surgeon exposes the hepatic artery, celiac trunk, takeoff of the splenic artery, and a portion of the portal vein. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the small bowel is divided using a blue-load stapler. The mesentery is then divided to create the Roux limb.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer with precision to maintain vascularization while freeing the bowel, ensuring it can reach the hiatus easily.

The surgeon uses Firefly technology to confirm the vascularity of the bowel. They measure 55 centimeters and create two small enterotomies to perform an enteroenterostomy using a blue load stapler. The anastomosis is confirmed, and the common channel is closed with a running 3-0 V-Loc suture using the Cornell technique, ensuring the anastomosis is not narrowed.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect created during the formation of the Roux limb, using sutures to secure the tissue and prevent internal herniation.
The surgeon carefully places the Orville device into the divided esophagus using robotic scissors, ensuring precision to avoid injury to the esophageal tissue.

The surgeon uses a size 25 anvil to remove the staple line from the prelim and places an extra-long size XXV EEA stapler. The anastomosis between the esophagus and the bowel is created, ensuring the bowel is not twisted during the procedure.

The surgeon removes the EEA device and checks the completeness of the anastomotic donuts. Subsequently, the bowel is divided using a blue load stapler, positioned three centimeters from the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an endoscopy and a leak test, followed by the placement of an isogastric tube under direct visualization. A surgical drain is then placed in the operative field.

to cover both the posterior mediastinum as well as the divided duodenum. The operation took approximately five hours. The ABL was fifty mLs. The patient was discharged home on postoperative day number five on a full liquid diet and no feeding agent was performed.
The pathology as stated with thirty negative lymph nodes. Thank you very much for your time and this is our team at Moffitt.
If you have any additional questions, please feel free to contact us.
